column1: # This is what PCA uses: each row is a genomic bin, each column is a chromatin state in one sample
# Epilogos 15-state model example with 3 samples across 2 regions
# Each column = chromatin state in one sample
# Values = KL divergence scores (higher = more confident in that state)
# ===== WHAT THIS LOOKS LIKE IN THE PCA =====
# The PCA flattens this into one big matrix:
# Each SAMPLE becomes a single point in PCA space (averaged across all bins and states)
# PC1 and PC2 capture the biggest variation across samples
# Example: 3 samples produce 3 points on the PCA plot
#   - sample2 → point B (perhaps upper-left if state2 is enriched)
#   - sample3 → point C (perhaps lower-right if state1 is enriched)
# The ARROWS show which states matter most:
#   - A long arrow pointing toward sample3 for "state1" = "state1 is what makes sample3 different"
#   - Short arrow for "state5" = "state5 barely changes between samples, ignore it"
# Notice in the example above: region2 has MUCH higher state1 across all samples
#   - All samples similar → would cluster together on PCA
#   - Sample3 has even MORE state1 (0.73 vs 0.53-0.63)
#   - This DIFFERENCE drives the PCA separation!
# If you use --pca-nonzero-only, bins where ALL states are near zero are removed
# In this example, all bins have at least one state >0.5, so they'd all be kept
# But in real sparse data, you might have bins like:
# Those would be EXCLUDED from PCA calculation when using --pca-nonzero-only
# This removes "background noise" and highlights signal-only differences
